questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by its index?
questions.explanation: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations. Accessing by index is a direct memory lookup, which takes constant time O(1).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of an array?
questions.explanation: Inserting at the beginning requires shifting all existing elements one position to the right, which takes O(n) time in the worst case.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of deleting an element from the end of an array?
questions.explanation: Deleting from the end doesn't require shifting any elements — just reducing the size by 1, which is O(1).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching for an element in an unsorted array?
questions.explanation: In the worst case, you may need to check every element. Linear search through an unsorted array takes O(n) time.

questions.question: In a sorted array, what is the time complexity of searching using Binary Search?
questions.explanation: Binary Search repeatedly halves the search space. It has O(log n) time complexity because the search space is divided by 2 at each step.

questions.question: What is the prerequisite for using Binary Search on an array?
options: The array must have unique elements
The array must have an even number of elements
The array must contain only integers
questions.explanation: Binary Search requires the array to be sorted. It compares the target with the middle element and eliminates half the array based on the ordering.

questions.question: What does O(1) time complexity mean?
options: The running time is constant regardless of input size
The algorithm uses one unit of memory
questions.explanation: O(1) means the operation takes constant time — it doesn't depend on the size of the input. The time remains the same whether n is 10 or 10 million.

questions.question: What does O(n) time complexity mean?
options: The running time grows linearly with the input size
The algorithm runs in constant time
The algorithm runs in logarithmic time
The algorithm uses n units of memory
questions.explanation: O(n) means the running time grows proportionally with the input size. If n doubles, the time approximately doubles.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Bubble Sort in the worst case?
questions.explanation: Bubble Sort compares adjacent elements and swaps them if out of order. In the worst case, it needs n passes over n elements: O(n²).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Merge Sort in all cases?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort divides the array in half recursively (log n levels) and merges them in O(n) time at each level. Total: O(n log n) for all cases.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of Merge Sort?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort requires auxiliary space proportional to the array size for merging. Its space complexity is O(n).

questions.question: What is the average time complexity of Quick Sort?
questions.explanation: With a good pivot selection, Quick Sort divides the array roughly in half each time, giving O(n log n) average-case performance.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of Quick Sort?
questions.explanation: If the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g., already sorted array with first/last element as pivot), Quick Sort degrades to O(n²).

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has the best worst-case time complexity among comparison-based sorts?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n) in all cases. Heap Sort also achieves this. Quick Sort can be O(n²) in the worst case.

questions.question: What is a Hash Map (Hash Table) primarily used for?
options: Storing key-value pairs with fast lookup
questions.explanation: Hash Maps store key-value pairs and use a hash function to compute an index, enabling O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion.

questions.question: What is the average-case time complexity of lookup in a Hash Map?
questions.explanation: With a good hash function and load factor, Hash Map lookups are O(1) on average because the hash directly points to the bucket.

questions.question: What is a collision in a Hash Map?
options: When two keys have the same hash value
When the hash map is full
When a key is not found
questions.explanation: A collision occurs when two different keys produce the same hash value, mapping to the same bucket. Common resolution methods: chaining and open addressing.

questions.question: What is Separate Chaining for collision resolution?
options: Storing colliding elements in a linked list at each bucket
questions.explanation: Separate Chaining stores all elements that hash to the same bucket in a linked list (or another data structure) at that bucket.

questions.question: What is the load factor of a Hash Map?
options: The ratio of occupied buckets to total buckets
questions.explanation: Load factor = number of elements / number of buckets. When it exceeds a threshold (e.g., 0.75), the hash map is typically resized.

questions.question: What is the Two Pointers technique used for?
options: Solving problems involving pairs in sorted arrays, such as finding two elements that sum to a target
questions.explanation: Two Pointers uses two indices moving toward each other (or same direction) to efficiently solve problems like two-sum in sorted arrays or palindrome checking.

questions.question: When is the Two Pointers technique most effective?
options: With sorted arrays or when traversing from both ends
questions.explanation: Two Pointers works best on sorted arrays or when you can traverse from both ends toward the center, often achieving O(n) instead of O(n²).

questions.question: What is the Sliding Window technique?
options: A technique for hash map resizing
A technique for solving subarray problems by maintaining a window that slides over the array
questions.explanation: Sliding Window maintains a contiguous subarray (window) that expands/contracts as it moves through the array, useful for finding maximum/minimum subarrays of a condition.

questions.question: What is the typical time complexity of a well-implemented Sliding Window algorithm?
questions.explanation: Each element is visited at most twice (once by the left pointer, once by the right), giving O(n) time complexity for most sliding window problems.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Insertion Sort in the best case?
questions.explanation: When the array is already sorted, Insertion Sort only needs one comparison per element: O(n) best case.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Insertion Sort in the worst case?
questions.explanation: When the array is reverse sorted, each element must be compared with all previous elements: O(n²) worst case.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm is considered stable?
questions.explanation: A stable sort preserves the relative order of equal elements. Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, and Bubble Sort are stable. Quick Sort and Heap Sort are not naturally stable.

questions.question: What does it mean for a sorting algorithm to be 'in-place'?
options: It uses no memory at all
It creates a copy of the array
It only works on small arrays
It sorts the data without requiring extra space proportional to input size (O(1) auxiliary space)
questions.explanation: In-place sorting modifies the original array. Quick Sort and Insertion Sort are in-place. Merge Sort is not (requires O(n) extra space).

questions.question: What is the space complexity of an iterative Binary Search implementation?
questions.explanation: Iterative Binary Search uses only a few variables (low, high, mid) regardless of input size, giving O(1) space complexity.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of a recursive Binary Search implementation?
questions.explanation: Recursive Binary Search makes log n recursive calls in the worst case, and each call adds a frame to the call stack: O(log n) space.

questions.question: What is the best-case time complexity of Quick Sort?
questions.explanation: When the pivot always divides the array into two roughly equal halves, Quick Sort achieves O(n log n) best-case performance.

questions.question: Which data structure is best for finding the frequency of elements in an array?
questions.explanation: A Hash Map is ideal for frequency counting — iterate once through the array and increment counts in O(n) time with O(n) space.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the 'Two Sum' problem using a Hash Map?
questions.explanation: Using a Hash Map, you can solve Two Sum in a single pass: for each element, check if (target - current) exists in the map (O(1) lookup), giving O(n) total time.

questions.question: What is the Dutch National Flag problem about?
options: Sorting an array of 0s, 1s, and 2s in linear time
Finding the median of three numbers
questions.explanation: The Dutch National Flag problem sorts an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s in O(n) time using three pointers — a classic application of the two-pointer technique.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding the maximum element in an unsorted array?
questions.explanation: You must examine every element at least once to find the maximum, which takes O(n) time.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding the k-th largest element using a Min-Heap of size k?
questions.explanation: Process each of n elements, maintaining a heap of size k. Each heap operation is O(log k), giving O(n log k) total time.

questions.question: What is an anagram?
options: A word that reads the same forwards and backwards
Two strings formed by rearranging the same characters
A word with no repeated letters
questions.explanation: Anagrams are words or phrases formed by rearranging the letters of another. 'listen' and 'silent' are anagrams.

questions.question: What is the most efficient way to check if two strings are anagrams?
options: Sort both strings and compare: O(n log n)
Use a frequency map (hash map) to count characters: O(n)
Check if they have the same length only
Compare character by character without sorting
questions.explanation: Using a frequency map, count characters in both strings and compare counts. This is O(n) time and O(1) space (for fixed character set).

questions.question: What is a palindrome string?
options: A string with all unique characters
A string that reads the same forwards and backwards
A string sorted in ascending order
questions.explanation: A palindrome reads identically from either direction. Examples: 'madam', 'racecar', 'level'.

questions.question: How can you check if a string is a palindrome optimally?
options: Use two pointers from both ends moving toward the center: O(n)
Reverse the string and compare using O(n) extra space
questions.explanation: Two pointers from both ends is optimal — O(n) time and O(1) extra space, comparing characters until they meet or cross.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of reversing a string?
questions.explanation: Reversing a string requires visiting each character once, which takes O(n) time where n is the length of the string.

questions.question: What is the Boyer-Moore voting algorithm used for?
options: Finding the majority element (appearing more than n/2 times) in O(n) time and O(1) space
questions.explanation: The Boyer-Moore voting algorithm finds the majority element by maintaining a candidate and a count, cancelling out non-matching pairs.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Kadane's Algorithm for finding the maximum subarray sum?
questions.explanation: Kadane's Algorithm traverses the array once, maintaining the maximum sum ending at each position. It runs in O(n) time.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of Kadane's Algorithm?
questions.explanation: Kadane's Algorithm uses only a few variables (current_max, global_max) regardless of input size, giving O(1) space.

questions.question: What is the difference between O(n) and O(2n)?
options: They are the same in Big O notation — constants are dropped
questions.explanation: Big O notation drops constant factors. O(2n) simplifies to O(n) because we care about growth rate, not exact multiples.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Selection Sort?
questions.explanation: Selection Sort finds the minimum element in each pass and places it at the beginning. It always performs n(n-1)/2 comparisons: O(n²).

questions.question: Is Selection Sort stable?
options: No, it can swap non-adjacent elements, changing the relative order of equal elements
questions.explanation: Selection Sort is not stable. Swapping elements that are far apart can change the relative order of equal elements.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Counting Sort?
options: It works for any data type
It sorts in O(n + k) time where k is the range of values, making it linear for small ranges
questions.explanation: Counting Sort is a non-comparison sort with O(n + k) time complexity. It's extremely fast when the range k is not significantly larger than n.

questions.question: What is the limitation of Counting Sort?
options: It only works for negative numbers
It requires knowing the range of input values in advance and uses O(k) extra space
It only works for floating-point numbers
questions.explanation: Counting Sort needs to know the range of values to allocate the count array. If the range k is very large, it becomes inefficient in space.

questions.question: When applying the Sliding Window technique, what determines when the window should shrink?
options: When the current window violates the problem's constraint (e.g., exceeds target sum or contains duplicates)
When the window reaches half the array size
When the left pointer equals the right pointer
questions.explanation: The window shrinks from the left when the current window no longer satisfies the problem constraints, then expands again from the right.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of comparisons needed in Linear Search for an array of size n?
questions.explanation: In the worst case, the target element is at the end or not present, requiring n comparisons. Average case is n/2, but Big O uses worst case: O(n).
